title: Leading Nutritionist Dr. Walter Willett Shares Tips for a ...
text:
welcome to BTR zoom my name is Margaret
flowers I'm the director of the research
program here at BC RF I am thrilled to
be joined today by dr. Walter Willett
professor of epidemiology and nutrition
at the Harvard th Chan School of Public
Health dr. Willard is the most cited
nutritionist in the world and a BCF
investigator for nearly 20 years dr.
Willa thank you so much for joining us
today to talk to us about how we can
continue to eat well while we're at home
during the Kovach crisis and I'd like to
begin by just asking you to very briefly
share with our audience the the nature
of your research well thanks Margaret
then it's really good to be able to talk
together even if we can't be there in
person and I really do want to express
my appreciation for all the support the
Breast Cancer Research Foundation has
given to our work over the last 20 or
more years now that's really made a huge
difference the work on breast cancer
that we conduct is what we call large
long term epidemiologic studies we begin
looking at risk factors for breast
cancer back in the 1970s and since 1980
diet has been a very central part of the
research that we do learning first at
what aspects of diet and lifestyle might
be related to lower risks or higher risk
of breast cancer and more recently know
that we've been able to attract women
who have developed breast cancer and
follow them for another couple of
decades we're looking at factors that
can influence survival after breast
cancer so this involves the work of many
different people on our team involves
laboratory measurements of hormones
nutrients as well as dietary data
getting the biological samples from
tumors this is from over 200,000 women
we've been tracking now and now over
several generations so it's given us I
think it's fair to say the most detailed
long-term look at factors that influence
risk of breast cancer and overall
well-being so we brought you
we asked you to join us today to sort of
share some of your knowledge on you know
what we can be doing and you know
especially now when we're trying to
minimize our trips to the grocery store
but we still want to we still want to
have fruits and vegetables as it as is
you know highly recommended and I wonder
if you could make any recommendations of
any particularly nutrient-dense produce
that might have a longer shelf life or
maybe ways that we can sort of extend
the shelf life of some fruits and
vegetables that we might normally buy
you know buy more frequently well that's
a very important question because now is
the time more than ever to be eating a
healthy diet that's balanced and foods
that will promote health and well-being
that's of course always a good thing to
be doing but in the event that we are in
some ways exposed to the Cova virus we
want to be in the best possible physical
shape and to be the most resilient it's
not a by accident that the factors that
are related most funny to adverse
outcomes outside of age are obesity
diabetes heart disease and hypertension
and these are all strongly related to
nutritional factors so this is a it's
challenging more than usual to make sure
that our diets are well balanced in
healthy and there are four findings as
you say some foods that have a longer
shelf life and really can help sustain a
really good diet are important for
staples foods like whole grains brown
rice for example quinoa whole grain
pasta our basic healthy staples and
that's opposed to the refined grain
version of those which are not so health
promoting beans and lentils often get
lost in the American diet but they're
really healthy the staple of diets all
around the world good protein sources
lots of minerals and vitamins and they
of course have a long shelf life can be
used in many many different ways so
I think our family were I knew waster
you'd be using those twins that probably
would be good to do I'm all around no
matter what I'd like to include nuts and
peanut butter as staples sometimes
people don't think of those but they
have a long shelf life it's good to keep
nuts in the refrigerator or freezer to
preserve their freshness they're very
important and healthy oils sometimes you
don't think of those staples but that's
an important part of a healthy balanced
diet - like olive oil but canola oils so
I've you know pretty much all a liquid
vegetable oils are good so there's lots
of good options there with a long shelf
life as far as vegetables and fruits
those are a little more challenging they
do tend to be somewhat more perishable
but still many of these foods can last a
long time we think of fruits and
vegetables as being highly perishable
but basically before we had modern
refrigeration there were some basics
that kept us over the winter and those
would be like carrots sweet potatoes
winter squash cabbage a little bit
lesser extent broccoli our vegetables
for lunch shelf - those are really
tremendously good sources many
micronutrients and vegetables and and
phytochemicals and again they can be
used in many different ways
greens like spinach kale collards are
often a weak spot in many diets in fact
our surveys we find about half of
Americans have almost no greens and
their diet so again that's something no
matter whether we're having a pandemic
or not it's important to think about for
those foods fresh fresh vegetables fresh
versions of these may be more difficult
to obtain have a shorter health like a
shelf life but frozen is good as well
and tomatoes are also good but actually
canned are probably better the
micronutrients are absorbed better than
fresh tomatoes they don't really think
we need to be getting fresh tomatoes all
winter long and conversions can be made
into soups and many other dishes as well
and I might had lunch juice has been a
standard for decades and decades good
source of vitamin C that were learning
it contains lots of other things as well
that can be frozen if fresh is not
available so lots of good options there
and I think one maybe silver lining in
this challenge were having us that a lot
of people are being a little more
creative and putting together diets that
are both healthy and and sustainable
excellent yeah that is really good to
hear and and also you know with the
frozen foods being a good option too
that we could maybe stock up and keep in
the freezer and that might be another
option for you know some fruits and
vegetables that we would typically but
you know buy fresh a little bit more
frequently that's also good news you
know I want to touch on two you had
mentioned how important it is right now
that we're that we stay fit
nutritionally and physically fit so that
our bodies can resist any any exposures
that we might come in contact with
especially with the köppen 19 virus so
could you talk briefly about what is the
relationship between nutrition and
immunity how does what we affect how
well we're able to fight off infection
and viruses now we've learned a lot
about nutrition and immunity over the
years and the bigger picture that
emerges is that if we have gaps in one
of many different essential nutrients
whether it be vitamin C or zinc vitamin
A that will make us more susceptible to
infections or end up with more serious
forms of infection if we do get infected
so we want to make sure that we don't
have any gaps in our nutritional
defenses that's really the most
important thing and it doesn't mean that
because those are essential and critical
that just adding more is better it's
really I think analogy with a car for
example you've got to have all these
different parts and they all need to be
working if any one of them falls out or
isn't working the whole thing may not
work right but just adding another the
same part doesn't necessarily make it
better we need four wheels and England
is it gonna make it better sometimes it
makes it worse so at this point in time
people of course would like to have a
super food or a super nutrient and just
eat lots of that we don't really see
that we have that such a thing as a
super food that will make us super
immune but making sure that's why
balance and completeness of diet we go
back to that again and again it's so
important we want to make sure that
there's no big gaps
I would don't point out one specific
nutrient that I can get neglected and
that's vitamin D because our natural
source is actually not primarily diet
it's from sun exposure and we the
vitamin D the Sun hitting our skin
generates vitamin D and normally that's
how we get fish yes a little bit but
we're not really going to get much
vitamin D unless you eat a lot of fish
and so this is a precarious time for
vitamin D status for many people in part
because in the north it's wintertime we
don't get much exposure to the sunlight
and ultraviolet rays are so weak they
don't generate much vitamin D and now
we're told to be inside more of the time
and also missing out on sunlight and
very importantly people with darker skin
because the melanin protects the skin
and by and blocks largely blacks the
ultraviolet rays people with darker skin
have much lower levels of vitamin D than
people with lighter skin and that is
we've seen that severe vitamin D
deficiency is 17 times more likely to be
president in african-americans compared
to white Americans there's a big
difference it's just simply because the
difference in melanin is
and so that's an area where supplements
actually I think can play an important
role because it's we're not going to get
the vitamin D from diet yeah even if we
have a totally healthy diet we can be
deficient in deep are there other what's
your recommendation on other regular
supplementation that we might feel you
know we might feel we need you know is
just trying to boost our immunity are
there other nutrient supplementation's
that you might recommend during this
time particularly well even before this
pandemic I thought it makes sense for
most people unless they're eating a
really perfect diet to take a standard
multiple vitamin multi-mineral and
that's like a bit of an insurance policy
there so that we don't have any gaps in
our diet and I'm not talking about
expensive mega doses here I checked out
a standard brand-name multiple vitamin
multi mineral just on the web a couple
days ago it was it's $25 a year to get
the RDA levels of multiple vitamins
multi mineral so this is not very
expensive at all and that many people
even if their think they're eating a
healthy diet can have some gaps also
people over 50 often I'm not absorbing
their vitamin b12 very well because our
stomach isn't making enough acid to
promote absorption premenopausal women
are often low in iron because of
menstrual losses so just a standard
multiple vitamin multi mineral will take
care of those gaps and pretty
effectively so yeah I think that's like
an insurance policy it does not at all
replace a healthy diet but is something
just to be sure we don't have any gaps
and then is we were just talking about
vitamin D unless someone is really
pretty confident that they have adequate
vitamin D levels either having checked
their levels or Odinson a lot I think it
does make sense for most people to be
taking a vitamin D supplement there's
still a lot of
maybe disagreement discussion about what
the optimal dose is but I think about
2000 international units a day is a good
dose of almost most people up to a good
level that's well within the upper limit
of 4000 and for people with darker skin
going Corsica 4000 actually mean may
make sense as well again this does not
replace a healthy diet but for vitamin D
there actually is there's been a
meta-analysis of synthesis of many
randomised trials and it has been shown
to reduce infections to reduce
inspiratory reflect infections some of
those including different kinds of
corona viruses as well so there is some
precedent and that's why number of
people have suggested particularly in
the coronavirus era that vitamin D
supplement to make sure not low would be
good again not massively overdosing but
just to make sure we're not we don't
have a big gap in our immune system it
was interesting it was interesting and
that these trials that people had
adequate blood levels of vitamin D then
adding a supplement didn't do anything
but again a lot of people have low
levels and that's what we really wanted
to wait the last question I wanted to
ask you was if you know if there was
just one piece of advice that you could
share with us in terms of you know our
nutritional health what would that be
and it sounds to me from what I'm
hearing that it would be get your
vitamin D is is that is that what your
your nutrition advice would be if it was
just like one specific thing that people
could do for themselves to boost their
immunity and to stay healthy during this
pandemic well I would go back to my
first comment first of all even healthy
balanced diet so the best we possibly
can recognizing that for some people
it's gotten much more challenging than
it was both for the availability of the
food and a lot of people
lost their jobs and incomes and the
clusters are becoming a real issue for
many people and we're struggling to
avoid gaps for the for economic reasons
but apart from overall healthy diet yes
I would think taking a vitamin D
supplement unless we're really sure we
have adequate vitamin D would be a good
thing to do it particularly if you have
darker skin because that is blocking the
synthesis of vitamin D that we might
otherwise get
